content.text: Groups in Microsoft Entra ID are containers that collect users, service principals, and other groups as members. They serve as the unit of access assignment — instead of granting permissions to individual users, you assign permissions to a group and manage membership. Entra ID supports three group types with distinct capabilities.

attrs.alt: Entra ID group types: Security Group, Microsoft 365 Group, and Distribution List

content.text: The primary vehicle for access control in Azure. Security groups can be assigned Azure RBAC roles, Entra ID directory roles, app roles, Conditional Access policy scope, and Microsoft 365 licenses. They have no email address and no associated workloads (mailbox, Teams, SharePoint).

A collaboration unit that bundles a shared mailbox, calendar, SharePoint site, Teams team, Planner board, and OneNote notebook. A Microsoft 365 group can also be security-enabled, which lets it serve double duty as an access-control group and a collaboration hub. Created automatically when a new Teams team is provisioned.

Email-only groups managed in Exchange. They cannot be used for Azure RBAC, app roles, or Conditional Access. For new implementations, prefer Microsoft 365 Groups which provide the same email routing plus collaboration features.

import requests

new_group = {
    "displayName": "sg-azure-contributors",
    "description": "Members receive Contributor on the production subscription",
    "mailEnabled": False,
    "securityEnabled": True,
    "mailNickname": "sg-azure-contributors",
    "groupTypes": []  # empty = assigned membership security group
}

resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/groups",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json=new_group
)
group = resp.json()
print("Group ID:", group["id"])
print("Display name:", group["displayName"])

dynamic_group = {
    "displayName": "sg-engineering-dynamic",
    "mailEnabled": False,
    "securityEnabled": True,
    "mailNickname": "sg-engineering-dynamic",
    "groupTypes": ["DynamicMembership"],
    "membershipRule": "(user.department -eq \"Engineering\") and (user.accountEnabled -eq True)",
    "membershipRuleProcessingState": "On"
}

resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/groups",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json=dynamic_group
)
print(resp.status_code, resp.json().get("id"))
# Membership evaluation starts asynchronously; check membershipRuleProcessingState
# until it returns "On" (active) or "Paused" (error in rule)

m365_group = {
    "displayName": "team-platform-engineering",
    "description": "Platform Engineering team workspace",
    "mailEnabled": True,
    "securityEnabled": False,
    "mailNickname": "team-platform-engineering",
    "groupTypes": ["Unified"],   # "Unified" = Microsoft 365 group
    "visibility": "Private"      # Private | Public | HiddenMembership
}

resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/groups",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json=m365_group
)
group = resp.json()
print("Group ID:", group["id"])
print("Mail:", group.get("mail"))    # auto-assigned: team-platform-engineering@contoso.com

# transitiveMemberOf returns all groups (including nested) a user belongs to
USER_ID = "<user-object-id>"
all_groups = requests.get(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{USER_ID}/transitiveMemberOf",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"},
    params={"$select": "displayName,id,securityEnabled"}
).json().get("value", [])

security_groups = [g for g in all_groups if g.get("securityEnabled")]
print(f"Member of {len(security_groups)} security groups (including nested):")
for g in security_groups[:10]:
    print(f"  {g['displayName']}")
Assigning Azure RBAC Roles to Groups

Assigning roles to groups rather than individual users is the recommended practice. A group assignment means adding/removing the user from the group is the only action needed when their access changes — no RBAC role assignments to hunt down across subscriptions.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.authorization import AuthorizationManagementClient
import uuid

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
auth_client = AuthorizationManagementClient(credential, "<subscription-id>")

# Contributor role definition ID (built-in, same across all tenants)
CONTRIBUTOR_ROLE_DEF = "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c"
GROUP_OBJECT_ID = "<group-object-id>"
SCOPE = "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-production"

assignment = auth_client.role_assignments.create(
    scope=SCOPE,
    role_assignment_name=str(uuid.uuid4()),
    parameters={
        "properties": {
            "role_definition_id": CONTRIBUTOR_ROLE_DEF,
            "principal_id": GROUP_OBJECT_ID,
            "principal_type": "Group"
        }
    }
)
print("Role assignment ID:", assignment.id)

# CLI: assign Contributor to a group on a resource group
GROUP_ID=$(az ad group show --group "sg-azure-contributors" --query id -o tsv)

az role assignment create     --assignee-object-id "$GROUP_ID"     --assignee-principal-type Group     --role Contributor     --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-production"

# List all group role assignments on a subscription
az role assignment list     --subscription "<sub-id>"     --query "[?principalType=='Group'].{Group:principalName,Role:roleDefinitionName,Scope:scope}"     -o table

Without governance, groups accumulate in the tenant — created for a project, then abandoned with lingering access. Entra ID supports group expiration policies (P1) that automatically expire groups after a configurable period unless renewed by an owner.

import requests

expiration_policy = {
    "groupLifetimeInDays": 180,
    "managedGroupTypes": "Selected",   # "All" or "Selected" or "None"
    "alternateNotificationEmails": "admin@contoso.com"
}

resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/groupLifecyclePolicies",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json=expiration_policy
)
print(resp.status_code, resp.json().get("id"))

# Add specific groups to the policy
POLICY_ID = resp.json()["id"]
GROUP_ID  = "<group-object-id>"

requests.post(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/groupLifecyclePolicies/{POLICY_ID}/addGroup",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json={"groupId": GROUP_ID}
)

# Renew a group that is about to expire (owner action)
requests.post(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/groups/{GROUP_ID}/renew",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"}
)
Bulk Group Audit: Finding Empty or Ownerless Groups
Large tenants accumulate empty groups and groups with no designated owner. Both are risks: empty groups waste RBAC slots; ownerless groups have no one accountable for membership decisions.
Assign RBAC to groups, not users
Group-based assignments scale — changing access = changing group membership, not hunting role assignments
Use security groups for RBAC, M365 groups for collaboration
Mixing the two leads to teams channels with RBAC side-effects or RBAC groups triggering mailbox provisioning
Use the 'Validate rules' tool in Entra admin center to test a rule against a specific user before saving
Azure RBAC resolves transitive group membership; Entra ID app roles do NOT — a user must be a direct member of the app role-assigned group
Always assign at least two owners per group; ownerless groups become unmanageable after the creator leaves
Distribution lists cannot hold service principals
If you need to email-notify a group that includes an app identity, use a security-enabled M365 group with a service principal member

Group-based licensing (P1) lets you assign Microsoft 365 and Entra ID licenses to a group. Every member automatically receives the license; when they leave the group the license is reclaimed. This makes license management a consequence of group membership.
Assigning a License to a Group

import requests

GROUP_ID = "<group-object-id>"
P2_SKU_ID = "84a661c4-e949-4bd2-a560-ed7766fcaf2b"
E5_SKU_ID = "06ebc4ee-1bb5-47dd-8120-11324bc54e06"
TEAMS_PLAN_ID = "57ff2da0-773e-42df-b2af-ffb7a2317929"

resp = requests.post(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/groups/{GROUP_ID}/assignLicense",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json={
        "addLicenses": [
            {"skuId": P2_SKU_ID, "disabledPlans": []},
            {"skuId": E5_SKU_ID, "disabledPlans": [TEAMS_PLAN_ID]}
        ],
        "removeLicenses": []
    }
)
print(resp.status_code)  # 202 Accepted

Group naming policies enforce consistent naming across the tenant — mandatory prefixes by department attribute, blocked words, and suffix patterns. This prevents ad hoc names like 'test' or 'my group' from cluttering the directory.

import requests

TEMPLATE_ID = "62375ab9-6b52-47ed-826b-58e47e0e304b"  # Group.Unified template

naming_policy = {
    "templateId": TEMPLATE_ID,
    "values": [
        {"name": "PrefixSuffixNamingRequirement", "value": "[Department]GRP-[GroupName]"},
        {"name": "CustomBlockedWordsList", "value": "CEO|Board|Confidential|Secret"},
        {"name": "EnableMSStandardBlockedWords", "value": "true"}
    ]
}

resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/groupSettings",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json=naming_policy
)
print(resp.status_code, resp.json().get("id"))
# Creating a group named "Engineering Team" for a user in the Engineering
# department produces: "EngineeringGRP-Engineering Team"
Diff-Based Membership Sync from External Systems

When your authoritative source for group membership is an HR system or project tool, a diff-based sync keeps Entra ID in lock-step: compute desired state, compute current state, apply adds and removes only where needed.

Access Reviews (P2 / ID Governance) schedule periodic reviewer-driven recertification campaigns for group membership. Default decision of Deny with auto-apply means untouched memberships are automatically removed at the end of the review period.

import requests
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

# Create a quarterly access review for a privileged group
review = {
    "displayName": "Q3 2026 sg-azure-admins Review",
    "startDateTime": datetime.now(timezone.utc).strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"),
    "endDateTime": (datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(days=14)).strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"),
    "reviewers": [{"query": "/groups/<group-id>/owners", "queryType": "MicrosoftGraph"}],
    "scope": {
        "query": "/groups/<group-id>/transitiveMembers",
        "queryType": "MicrosoftGraph"
    },
    "settings": {
        "mailNotificationsEnabled": True,
        "defaultDecisionEnabled": True,
        "defaultDecision": "Deny",        # auto-deny if reviewer ignores
        "autoApplyDecisionsEnabled": True,
        "recommendationsEnabled": True    # AI recommendations shown to reviewer
    }
}

resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityGovernance/accessReviews/definitions",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json=review
)
print(resp.json().get("id"))

For complex project roles that require membership in multiple groups simultaneously, Entra ID Governance Entitlement Management bundles groups, apps, and SharePoint sites into an access package that users can self-request and managers can approve.
Named bundle of resources (groups, apps, SharePoint sites, roles)
Defines who can request, who approves, and when access expires
Container for access packages; defines which resources can be included
User self-service request or admin direct grant
Active grant of the access package to a specific user with expiry

Entitlement Management replaces email-based approval workflows and provides a complete audit trail of who requested, who approved, and when access expired — reducing manual error and uncontrolled access growth.

Entra ID groups are the primary unit of access management. Security groups control Azure RBAC, app roles, and Conditional Access scope; Microsoft 365 Groups provision collaboration workloads alongside access control; Distribution Lists handle email routing only.
Use security groups for RBAC assignments — assign the group to a role, then manage who has that role by managing group membership
Dynamic groups (P1) eliminate manual membership management for predictable patterns like all users in a department
Add members in bulk with the members@odata.bind property (up to 20 per PATCH request)
Check transitive membership with /users/{id}/transitiveMemberOf — Entra ID app roles require direct membership, not nested
Group expiration policies (P1) prevent abandoned groups from accumulating access indefinitely
Always assign at least two owners per group to prevent orphaning
